full_text: Hi guys, my name is Nitish and you're welcome to my youtube channel In this video we will continue our chain playlist Today's topic is Chains Chains is an important topic in the chain I have already told you that this topic is so important that the name of the chain is written on it So today's topic is going to be taught in two videos In today's video I will teach you the fundamentals of chains And I will show you different types of chains On the whole it's a very interesting video Because you will learn some things that you will use in the chain So please make sure you watch this video end to end Let's start the video So guys before starting the video I would like to give you a small recap of what we have covered in this playlist So in this playlist we have discussed two important components of the chain The first component we discussed was the model component Where we learned how to interact with different types of AI models After that we discussed the second component which was prompts Where we learned how to send inputs to LLMs from different types And apart from this we learned one more very important thing Which is not exactly a component of the chain but is a very important part And that is how you can generate structured output from your LLM And in the process of generating structured output we learned the concept of output parsers So this has been our progress so far in this playlist Now with today's video we will start reading a new component of the chain In fact today's video and the next video are both about the chain component Which is again a very important component of the chain So in today's video we will see the fundamentals of chains I will tell you what chains are, why they are needed And then we will learn to create different types of chains And in the next video I will teach you the working behind the chains So there is a very important concept in the chain which we call runnable We will learn how to create the runnable concept in the next video So both these videos are going to be very important So now that I have given you a high level overview of what we are going to do in this video Now let's start this video So guys, first we will discuss in simple words what chains are and why they are needed So if you are using LLMs to create applications You will notice that your application is made by taking small steps For example, we talk about a simple application where you are taking a prompt from the user You are sending it to LLM and then you are showing the response to the user It is such a simple application There are three steps in this too So the first step is where you are asking for a prompt from the user Step number two is when you are sending that prompt to LLM And step number three is when the response of LLM comes back You are processing it and showing it to the user in the output So basically, whenever you will create any LLM based application There will be multiple smaller steps that you will add to the application Now the problem is that if you want to execute all these steps individually Then you might need a lot of effort As we have done so far What we do is design prompts using something like prompt template Then we ask for input from the user with the help of invoke function This gives us our prompt Now we have to send this prompt to LLM So we call the LLM.invoke function and manually put the prompt inside LLM Then LLM sends us the output Then we take out the content part from the output and show it to the user So the way we have used to build this application is very manual Where we are making every component and handling it ourselves Now this is a problem if you want to make a very big and complex application This is where chains come into the picture Chains are a way to create a pipeline Basically, you can do all the small steps in your application Like prompt designing, sending input to LLM And then processing the output and displaying it You can connect all these steps and make a pipeline The biggest feature of this pipeline is that Automatically, the output of the first step works for the second step And the output of the second step works for the third step And so on Now, as soon as you have made this pipeline You have to input the input in the first step And trigger this pipeline As soon as this pipeline is triggered, the first step will be executed automatically The input will come out from here And it will automatically become an input for the second step Then the second step will be triggered automatically And the output that will come out here will automatically become an input for the next step Then the next step will be triggered By doing this, your entire pipeline will be executed automatically And at the end you will get your output So you don't have to manually work with all these components and steps You have built the pipeline And input the first step The pipeline is executed and you get the final step output And by doing this, your work becomes very easy And this is the main reason why you use chains And this is not the best thing about the chain There are better things than this That this is just one way to make a pipeline Where I have made a linear or sequential pipeline You can use the chains to make pipelines of different structures Suppose you want parallel processing So you can actually do parallel processing Meaning you can create parallel chains Not only that, based on a condition You can make conditional chains if you want Meaning you got some input here You put a condition on it And on the basis of different conditions, you can execute different chains You can also make very complex types of chains In order to build very complex LLM applications So in today's video, we will do the same I will show you three different types of chains The first one is the sequential chain Where we will connect the steps in series And execute the task by making a pipeline Then I will show you a parallel chain Where we will execute two chains parallel So we can do more than two I will show you a third, conditional chain Where based on a condition, we will execute different chains So if you want to learn these three types of chains Trust me, after that you can build many complex LLM applications in a jiffy But before making these three types of chains I want to show you a simple chain How a simple chain looks like So let's work on that So let's go guys We will make a very simple chain This chain will be of three steps In step 1, we will ask for a prompt from the user In step 2, we will send it to an LLM In step 3, we will display the response from LLM And we will do all this with the help of chains So first of all we will have to import certain libraries So we will import chat openai from langchain.opinai And from .env we will import load.env From langchain.corp.prompt we will import prompt template First of all, we will load .env After that we will make our prompt with the help of prompt template So we will ask the user for a topic in this application And we will generate 5 interesting facts on that topic So our prompt template will look like this So we will write template Generate 5 interesting facts about topic Topic is our variable We will come down here and we will put the topic in input variables This is our prompt After that we will make our model We will call chat openai function which is the default model It will automatically apply You can also put your favorite model And lastly we need a parser that will take us to output string format And I had taught you in the last lecture that the parser you use is called string output parser So we will go to output parser and import string output parser And here we will make an object of string output parser So at this point I have defined all my steps I have my prompt, model and parser Now I will join all these steps and form my chain So I will write chain is equal to prompt pipe operator And this is the way to form chain where you attach pipe operator and join components This is called Langen expression language We will discuss about this in detail in the next video But for now this is the syntax that we are going to use So we made a prompt and connected our model to it And then we connected our parser to the model So this is a declarative syntax to tell that my pipeline looks like this It starts with prompt, then comes model, then comes parser Now that you have made your chain and pipeline Now you simply have to invoke it Invoking means triggering And you just have to provide the input required for the first step So you have to provide the input required for prompt Which is your topic So suppose I have given the topic cricket Now what will happen is first of all this input will go to the prompt The invoke function inside the prompt will automatically call And we will get our prompt What will be the prompt? Generate 5 interesting facts about this topic cricket Then we will send this prompt to the model The invoke function of the model will automatically call From there we will get some response There will be a lot of metadata in response We will take all that and send it to the parser The invoke function of the parser will call or the pars method will call And we will get only the string after turning it So now what I can do is I can store the result in a variable called result And I can print whatever is inside the result And that's it guys This is our simple chain example We will run this And you can see this is our output Now if you have seen the previous lectures Then you must be understanding how simple our work is by using chains First we used to invoke the prompt separately Invoke the model separately Remove the content from the result separately We used to remove the content from the result separately There is no need to do all this Everything is executed through this single line And the fun part is that you can visualize your chain In fact I will show you What you can do is you can print your chain and see what steps are there So for this there is a function called getGraph You can call getGraph And you can call the function printAskey in it This will help you visualize your chain And you can see This is the chain Prompt input came It filled the prompt template From there we called our model By sending the prompt The output came It went to string output parser And then we can see the output of string output parser And you can see this is our chain This is a simple chain Now we will do one thing We will make a little more complex chain than this It will be sequential But in that particular chain We will call LLM twice We will discuss that next In this particular example We are making an application Where we will ask the user for a topic Like cricket The user will provide us that topic We will ask the user for a topic We will send that topic to an LLM With this prompt That we need a detailed report on that topic So this LLM will process that prompt And will make a detailed report After that we will send this detailed report Back to that LLM And we will tell a second prompt That we need to extract the five most important pointers from this detailed report So in a way we are summarizing our report So we need to make this particular application Now you can see in front That this is a little more complex than the previous example Because here you have to make two LLM calls It is also sequential Everything is executed step by step after one step But your chain is a little longer than before So we will make this particular application next So here I have made a new file And we have written the imports we need Now first of all I will design my prompt number 1 Which will be designed with the help of prompt template In template I will write Generate a detailed report on topic And here the topic will come in input variables This is my first prompt Similarly I will make my second prompt This will also be done with the help of prompt template And here my template will be done Given Or rather I should print it like this Generate a five pointer summary from the following text And I will change the line and provide the text here And here the text is in the input variables So these are my two prompts Prompt number 1 and 2 Now I need a model So the model will come from chat open ai And I need a parser Which I will get from string output parser And now we are ready to design our chain So our chain will start from prompt 1 As soon as the prompt 1 is formed We will send it to our model Our model will generate a response Which will be multiple things Text and metadata will also be there I will send it to our parser We will get our detailed report from parser And now it will work as text for our next step So here I will call prompt 2 Prompt 2 will generate this particular prompt Which I have to send back to the model The model will generate a response again Which I have to send back to the parser And that's it guys This is the chain Now I will invoke this chain So I will write Topic And we can write topic Anything Unemployment in India Suppose this is our topic So the response we are getting from the palette We are storing the result in a variable And we are printing out the result Save Run And you can see guys This is the output We got a 5 pointer summary As before If you want to see how your chain looks So again you would write Chain.getgraph.print as key Save Run See Prompt template was formed from the input Went to chatopaniai Went to parser Got the parser output Which we sent back to prompt template Then called chatopaniai Put parser and got the parser output Which we can see And this is how we can create No matter how long the sequential chain is I hope you understood this Now what we will do We will form a parallel chain So that is new You will enjoy doing that Now we will make an interesting application Where we will do User will give us a Text Text as in You can understand that You will get detailed text on any topic Suppose in the domain of machine learning We got a big document related to linear regression Where linear regression Is explained in detail What we will do We will take this document And We will generate two things from it First We will generate nodes from this document And second We will generate a quiz from this document And we will combine These two And show it to the user So this is the application That we are going to build To build this We will have to use parallel chains So I will tell you how So what will happen User will provide us That big document After this we have to generate two things We need nodes and we need a quiz So we can do both these things in parallel So what we will do We will send this text first in model 1 Suppose this is a model OpenAI model GPT It will generate Notes And at the same time Parallel We will take a second model Suppose this is Claude And with this We will generate a quiz And then what we will do We will send these two in a third model And We will merge these two And display it in output So this is going to be the Architecture of this application We are going to use parallel chains It looks complex But trust me When you will make it with the help of chains You will realize how simple it is So let's code So here I have made a file What I will do is I will copy and paste These imports because we have to use it again and again Now what I will do I will make two models First will be my model 1 Which will be My chat openAI model And second will be Chat Anthropic Let's import it too From Lang chain Underscore Anthropic Import Chat Anthropic Again you can use free models I have taught you And here you Will do chat Anthropic And in model name We have put Lord 3 Okay After that what we will do We will design our prompts So first of all We are making our first prompt Where we will say First model I am giving you a text You can turn me and generate its notes Okay So we will use prompt template class Our template will be Generate Short And simple Notes From the Following Text And By changing the line I will provide the Text Input variables I will pass text This is my first prompt Along with that I will create my second prompt So my second prompt Where I am giving my second model Text and saying To generate a quiz So Here will be template Generate Five Short Question, answers From the Following Text Line change Text Input variables Text This is my second prompt Now I will make my final prompt Which is prompt 3 Where We have to merge Both outputs So I will write in template I will write in template Merge The provided Notes And quiz Into The following Into A single document Slash N Here I will provide Notes Notes And Quiz And here Input variables Will be two One will be notes Second will be quiz These are my three prompts So models are ready Prompts are ready We just need one more thing Parcer So our string output will be parsed Parcer Now the main work starts from here So Your chain We will develop it in two parts In the first part We will develop Parallel chains And in the second part We will develop the merging part So this parallel Thing is one part And this merging thing Is one part And we have to merge these two and make a chain So first let's make a parallel chain Now to make a parallel chain You need one thing Which we call in the language of the chain Runnable parallel So I will import it With the chain.schema Runnable import Runnable parallel Now what is runnable parallel I will tell you So runnable parallel is a type of runnable Now I have not taught you runnables My plan is I will tell you in the next video In detail So my plan is I will teach you in detail about runnables For now just understand Runnable parallel Is a kind of runnable With the help of which You can Parallelize multiple chains Okay This definition is enough for now In the next class when I will teach you runnable The whole concept will be clear to you Okay So what we need to do is We need to make a parallel chain And this parallel chain will be made With the help of runnable parallel Okay Now in runnable parallel You can execute any number of parallel chains What you have to do is You have to provide a dictionary And inside the dictionary Both your chains Right now we are executing two parallel chains You have to give a name to both the chains Like we are calling our first chain as notes And how does this chain look like? This chain looks like You have a prompt 1 Which you are sending To model 1 And the output coming from model 1 You are sending it to parser This is your This chain This part Okay So we called this chain as notes And then what I will do Then what I will do Here I will make a second chain Which is called quiz Which will look like this The input which was received from prompt 2 I am sending it to merging chain Now by mixing parallel chain and merge chain You will make a final chain Which you will call chain And this chain is Parallel chain connected to Merge chain And now you can see the power will send an email To customer support Okay And customer support will Talk to the customer Okay So if you can make this then it is a good Application but unfortunately We have not taught you how to make agents What is the concept of tools So that is why we are keeping it simple That if sentiment is positive Then we are giving a positive response If sentiment is negative Then we are giving a response accordingly Okay so for now we are making this Application but going forward When we make agents there I will make something like this Main idea here is that I want to show you How to make conditional chains Okay So this chain will look like User's feedback will come And we will send it To a model The work of the model Is to extract sentiment Is it positive or negative Based on Is it positive or negative We will work further If it is positive Then we will send back Another model or the same model And will say to make a response Accordingly If it is negative Then we will send it to the same model And will say accordingly Generate a reply according to the negative response The special thing here is That from these two Paths only one path Will be executed Both will never be together Like it happens in parallel So this is called conditional chain So let's build this application So I have made a new file I will copy This much actually Because we need Imports again and make sure That the output here is consistent Positive means always positive Never small positive, capital positive This is a positive sentiment Just always positive Same for negative So what we have to do We have to structure its output To structure it We will use Pydantic output parser So we will write From lang chain Core dot Output parsers Import Pydantic output parser And There We will import Similarly with the help of runnable branch You can execute the chain By using if else Rest again how it works internally I will tell you in the next video So now what I will do is That we will make The second part of our chain I don't need to invoke this Now the classifier chain is working properly Now I will create the branch Chain Branch will be made With the help of runnable branch Now what you do in runnable branch That you Send multiple tuples Ok like this Send multiple tuples In first tuple As in in every tuple You pass two things First condition Second What chain you want to execute On the true condition Similarly Condition 2 And on the true condition What chain you want to execute On the true condition This is your format And lastly If your condition is not meeting Then you this chain Which chain? I will tell you Our chain is Prompt 2 To model And from model To parser 1 Or to parser Parser is string output parser So here see What happened is I am saying that if Sentiment value is positive Then you trigger this chain And if Sentiment value Is negative Then you Trigger this chain Prompt 3 Will go to model And from model Will go to parser Ok So this is my logic If sentiment is equal to equal Sentiment, so no processing is happening Input is getting this In output we are returning this There is only one problem That this is not a chain You have to execute a chain This is not a chain So here We have to do something We have to convert This particular lambda function Into a runnable Ok, now I know You might not understand all these things Because I am using the word runnable again and chunks.text: Hi guys, my name is Nitish and you're welcome to my youtube channel
In this video we will continue our chain playlist
Chains is an important topic in the chain
I have already told you that this topic is so important that the name of the chain is written on it
So today's topic is going to be taught in two videos
In today's video I will teach you the fundamentals of chains
And I will show you different types of chains
On the whole it's a very interesting video
Because you will learn some things that you will use in the chain
So please make sure you watch this video end to end
So guys before starting the video I would like to give you a small recap of what we have covered in this playlist
So in this playlist we have discussed two important components of the chain
The first component we discussed was the model component
Where we learned how to interact with different types of AI models
After that we discussed the second component which was prompts
Where we learned how to send inputs to LLMs from different types
And apart from this we learned one more very important thing
Which is not exactly a component of the chain but is a very important part
And that is how you can generate structured output from your LLM
And in the process of generating structured output we learned the concept of output parsers
So this has been our progress so far in this playlist
Now with today's video we will start reading a new component of the chain
In fact today's video and the next video are both about the chain component
Which is again a very important component of the chain
So in today's video we will see the fundamentals of chains
I will tell you what chains are, why they are needed
And then we will learn to create different types of chains
And in the next video I will teach you the working behind the chains
So there is a very important concept in the chain which we call runnable
We will learn how to create the runnable concept in the next video
So both these videos are going to be very important
So now that I have given you a high level overview of what we are going to do in this video
So guys, first we will discuss in simple words what chains are and why they are needed
So if you are using LLMs to create applications
You will notice that your application is made by taking small steps
For example, we talk about a simple application where you are taking a prompt from the user
You are sending it to LLM and then you are showing the response to the user
It is such a simple application
There are three steps in this too
So the first step is where you are asking for a prompt from the user
Step number two is when you are sending that prompt to LLM
And step number three is when the response of LLM comes back
You are processing it and showing it to the user in the output
So basically, whenever you will create any LLM based application
There will be multiple smaller steps that you will add to the application
Now the problem is that if you want to execute all these steps individually
Then you might need a lot of effort
As we have done so far
What we do is design prompts using something like prompt template
Then we ask for input from the user with the help of invoke function
Now we have to send this prompt to LLM
So we call the LLM.invoke function and manually put the prompt inside LLM
Then LLM sends us the output
Then we take out the content part from the output and show it to the user
So the way we have used to build this application is very manual
Where we are making every component and handling it ourselves
Now this is a problem if you want to make a very big and complex application
This is where chains come into the picture
Chains are a way to create a pipeline
Basically, you can do all the small steps in your application
Like prompt designing, sending input to LLM
And then processing the output and displaying it
You can connect all these steps and make a pipeline
The biggest feature of this pipeline is that
Automatically, the output of the first step works for the second step
And the output of the second step works for the third step
Now, as soon as you have made this pipeline
You have to input the input in the first step
As soon as this pipeline is triggered, the first step will be executed automatically
The input will come out from here
And it will automatically become an input for the second step
Then the second step will be triggered automatically
And the output that will come out here will automatically become an input for the next step
Then the next step will be triggered
By doing this, your entire pipeline will be executed automatically
And at the end you will get your output
So you don't have to manually work with all these components and steps
The pipeline is executed and you get the final step output
And by doing this, your work becomes very easy
And this is the main reason why you use chains
And this is not the best thing about the chain
There are better things than this
That this is just one way to make a pipeline
Where I have made a linear or sequential pipeline
You can use the chains to make pipelines of different structures
So you can actually do parallel processing
Meaning you can create parallel chains
Not only that, based on a condition
You can make conditional chains if you want
Meaning you got some input here
You put a condition on it
And on the basis of different conditions, you can execute different chains
You can also make very complex types of chains
In order to build very complex LLM applications
So in today's video, we will do the same
I will show you three different types of chains
The first one is the sequential chain
Where we will connect the steps in series
And execute the task by making a pipeline
Then I will show you a parallel chain
Where we will execute two chains parallel
So we can do more than two
I will show you a third, conditional chain
Where based on a condition, we will execute different chains
So if you want to learn these three types of chains
Trust me, after that you can build many complex LLM applications in a jiffy
But before making these three types of chains
